meta.description: Practice starts at 169 strategically distinct starting-hand classes. Combo-level drilling can later expand that to all 1,326 two-card combinations.

cbet.prompt: You opened the button and got called by the BB. Flop A-7-2 rainbow. What's the best practical default?
cbet.explanation: Dry ace-high board gives the preflop raiser a clear range and nut advantage. Small c-bet at high frequency is the clean default.

cbet.prompt: Cutoff opened, BB called. Flop K-8-3 rainbow. Default?
cbet.explanation: Another dry high-card board where your range leans stronger than the defender's. Small size keeps the whole range printing.

cbet.prompt: You 3-bet BTN vs CO and got called. Flop A-8-4 rainbow. Best default?
cbet.explanation: 3-bet pots on dry ace-high textures heavily favor the aggressor. Small sizing pressures misses and realizes equity cleanly.

cbet.prompt: Blind vs blind, SB opened and BB called. Flop Q-6-2 rainbow. Good default?
cbet.explanation: Even blind vs blind, this dry queen-high board still lets the opener lean into a cheap range c-bet.

cbet.prompt: Button opened, BB defended. Flop T-8-6 with two clubs. Best practical default?
cbet.explanation: This board smashes the BB's defend range. Too connected and too many pair+draw combos for mindless c-betting.

cbet.prompt: UTG opened, BB called. Flop 9-7-5 with a flush draw. What's the better default?
cbet.explanation: Low connected boards pull the defender closer to parity. Checking protects your range and avoids lighting money on fire.

cbet.prompt: Cutoff opened, BB called. Flop K-J-4 with a flush draw. Practical default?
cbet.explanation: Still enough top-card advantage to c-bet often, but use a controlled size instead of blasting into all the draws.

cbet.prompt: You 3-bet BTN vs HJ and get called. Flop Q-T-5 with a flush draw. Default?
cbet.explanation: Broadway connectivity plus flush draws gives the caller real coverage here. Checking more is the disciplined play.

cbet.prompt: BTN opened, BB defended. Flop A-J-9 with a heart draw. What's your best default?
cbet.explanation: This is closer than A-7-2, but you still keep a real ace-high advantage. Small size works better than over-correcting to a check.

cbet.prompt: Blind vs blind on 8-6-4 two-heart. Default?
cbet.explanation: BB smashes this board with pair+draw, straight draws, and combo draws. This is a classic check-more texture.

cbet.prompt: You 3-bet BTN vs CO and get called. Flop K-7-2 with a club draw. Best practical line?
cbet.explanation: You still retain a strong range edge in the 3-bet pot. The draw adds texture, but not enough to force a pure check.

cbet.prompt: Cutoff opened, BB defended. Flop J-9-8 with a flush draw. What's the disciplined default?
cbet.explanation: Very dynamic board, lots of two-pair, pair+draw, and combo-draw coverage for BB. This is not a board to auto-fire.

cbet.prompt: BTN opened, BB called. Flop T-9-3 rainbow. Default line?
cbet.explanation: You have range advantage but the board connects with BB's range. Small bet keeps equity without overplaying.

cbet.prompt: CO opened, BB called. Flop J-J-2. Default?
cbet.explanation: Paired board heavily favors the preflop raiser — you have all full houses and sets. Small bet at high frequency.

cbet.prompt: BTN opened, BB called. Flop 8-7-6 two-tone. Default?
cbet.explanation: Connected wet board smashes BB's calling range (suited connectors). Your range advantage is minimal — check and realize equity.

cbet.prompt: CO opened, BB called. Flop Q-9-4 rainbow. Default?
cbet.explanation: You have range advantage with QQ+ and Q-x. Small probe keeps the initiative.

cbet.prompt: BTN opened, BB called. Flop K-J-T rainbow. Best play?
cbet.explanation: Very coordinated broadway board — both ranges smash it. Check back and re-evaluate turn.

cbet.prompt: You 3-bet BTN, SB called OOP. Flop A-5-2 rainbow. Default?
cbet.explanation: In a 3-bet pot you dominate this dry ace-high board. Polarized small bet at high frequency.

cbet.prompt: You 3-bet from BB, BTN called. Flop 9-8-7 rainbow OOP. Default?
cbet.explanation: OOP in a 3-bet pot on a rundown board is dangerous. BTN's calling range has exactly these connectors. Check-call strong, check-fold marginal.

cbet.prompt: CO opened, BB called. Flop 6-5-4 monotone. Default?
cbet.explanation: Monotone connected boards are traps for the preflop raiser. BB connects with flush draws and straights. Check back.

cbet.prompt: BTN opened, BB called. Flop A-T-7 with a flush draw. Default?
cbet.explanation: Ace-high board with nut flush draws in your range. Small bet builds pot with range advantage.

cbet.prompt: You opened SB, BB called. Flop Q-T-5 with flush draw. You're OOP. Default?
cbet.explanation: SB vs BB OOP on a connected board with flush draw — BB's range hits this well. Check and respond to their action.

cbet.prompt: You 3-bet BTN vs BB. Flop K-Q-J rainbow. Default in 3-bet pot?
cbet.explanation: Both ranges smash this board — BB's 3-bet calling range has KQ, KJ, QJ, AK, AQ. Check and control pot.

cbet.prompt: UTG opened, BB called. Flop 4-4-2 rainbow. Default?
cbet.explanation: Dry low paired board — neither player likely has a 4. Strong range advantage for opener. Small bet entire range.

shoveFold.prompt: 8bb on the button, folded to you. What's the play?
shoveFold.explanation: Classic profitable button shove. Suited ace with blocker value and strong equity when called.

shoveFold.prompt: 7bb in the small blind, folded to you. Best move?
shoveFold.explanation: Blind-vs-blind at 7bb is an aggression tax spot. KTs is comfortably strong enough to rip.

shoveFold.prompt: 9bb in the cutoff, folded to you. What now?
shoveFold.explanation: Mid pairs at 9bb are textbook jams from late position. Too strong to pass, awkward to min-raise/fold.

shoveFold.prompt: 10bb in the hijack, folded to you. Jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: Too loose from HJ. Offsuit queen-rag doesn't have the blocker/equity mix needed at 10bb.

shoveFold.prompt: 11bb on the button, folded to you. What's better?
shoveFold.explanation: Still profitable enough on the button against normal blinds. Strong king blocker plus suitedness carries it.

shoveFold.prompt: 12bb in the cutoff, action folds to you. Jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: A5o uses ace blocker power well and still has acceptable called equity. Practical jam at this depth.

shoveFold.prompt: 9bb in the SB, folded to you. What's the move?
shoveFold.explanation: Blind-vs-blind widens fast. J7o is ugly, but still jams profitably at 9bb once BTN folds.

shoveFold.prompt: 12bb in the hijack, folded to you. Rip it or pass?
shoveFold.explanation: Too loose that early with 12bb. This hand looks prettier than it plays once called.

shoveFold.prompt: 14bb in the cutoff, action folds to you. Jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: This gets close, but small pairs still make money as open jams often enough at 14bb in late position.

shoveFold.prompt: 13bb on the button, folded to you. Ship it?
shoveFold.explanation: Borderline-but-good late-position jam. Suited queen with live-card equity gets over the line.

shoveFold.prompt: 11bb in the SB, folded to you. Jam or let it go?
shoveFold.explanation: Blind-vs-blind keeps this alive. Suiting plus immediate fold equity makes it a practical jam.

shoveFold.prompt: 10bb UTG, folded to you. Jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: Ace blocker is nice, but position matters. UTG jamming range is tighter and A4o falls just outside the comfort zone.

shoveFold.prompt: 7bb on the button, folded to you. T9s — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: T9s plays extremely well at this depth. Strong equity vs calling ranges. Slam it in.

shoveFold.prompt: 6bb, SB jams. You're in the BB with K4o. Call or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: At 6bb SB jams very wide. K4o has enough equity against that range — call.

shoveFold.prompt: 11bb in the CO, action folds to you. 55 — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: Mid pair at 11bb from CO is a clear jam. Often win blinds outright, solid equity when called.

shoveFold.prompt: 13bb UTG, first to act. A3o — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: Offsuit weak ace from UTG at 13bb is a fold. Jamming into 5 players with A3o is not profitable.

shoveFold.prompt: 14bb on BTN, CO min-raises. You have QTs. Best play?
shoveFold.explanation: QTs is too strong to call off ~28% of stack awkwardly. Jam for value and fold equity.

shoveFold.prompt: 15bb in the HJ, folded to you. A2s — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: Suited ace with nut blocker. Good equity when called. Solid 15bb jam from HJ.

shoveFold.prompt: 10bb, BTN jams. You're BB with J9s. Call or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: BTN shoves wide at 10bb (~50% of hands). J9s has ~46% equity vs that range. With ~2.5:1 pot odds, call.

shoveFold.prompt: 12bb in the SB, folded to you. K7o — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: HU vs BB at 12bb, K7o is a jam. Fold equity, strong blocker, reasonable equity when called.

shoveFold.prompt: 15bb UTG, folded to you. 88 — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: 88 at 15bb — min-raising and folding to a 3-bet is awkward. Just jam and take the money or coin-flip with solid equity.

shoveFold.prompt: 10bb BTN, folded to you. J4s — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: At 10bb from BTN jam ~45% of hands. J4s is borderline in — jack blocker + suitedness gets it there.

shoveFold.prompt: 8bb CO, folded to you. K2o — jam or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: K2o from CO at 8bb lacks enough equity when called. Offsuit weak kings need better support from middle position.

shoveFold.prompt: 8bb, UTG jams. You're BB with ATo. Call or fold?
shoveFold.explanation: ATo has ~56% equity vs UTG 8bb jam range. With pot odds and 1bb invested, easy call.

potOdds.prompt: Villain bets $50 into a $100 pot. What equity % do you need to call?
potOdds.explanation: Call $50 into $150 total. 50/150 = 33%. You need at least 33% equity to break even.

potOdds.prompt: Villain bets $100 into a $100 pot (pot-sized). Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 100/300 = 33%. A pot-sized bet always requires 33% equity — memorize this anchor.

potOdds.prompt: Villain bets $50 into a $200 pot (quarter pot). Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 50/250 = 20%. Small bets give you great odds — only need 20% equity.

potOdds.prompt: Pot is $120, villain bets $80 (~67% pot). Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 80/200 = 40%. Bigger bets demand more equity. Two-thirds pot = need 40%.

potOdds.prompt: Villain bets $75 into $100 (~3/4 pot). Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 75/175 = 43%. Three-quarter pot bets price out most draws — 8-out draws only have ~32%.

potOdds.prompt: Villain overbets $300 into a $200 pot. Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 300/500 = 60%. Overbets require serious equity — why big overbets usually represent the near-nuts.

potOdds.prompt: You have 9h8h on 7c6dAs. Open-ended straight draw. How many outs?
potOdds.explanation: OESD = 8 outs (four 5s + four Ts). Rule of 2: ~16% per street. Rule of 4: ~32% for two streets.

potOdds.prompt: You have Ah2h on Kh9h3c. Flush draw. How many outs?
potOdds.explanation: Flush draw = 9 outs (13 in suit - 4 seen). Rule of 4: ~36% over two streets.

potOdds.prompt: You have JhTh on Kh9h2c. Flush draw + gutshot to Q. Combined clean outs?
potOdds.explanation: 9 flush outs + 4 queens (gutshot) = 13 minus QH already counted in flush = 12 clean outs. Rule of 4: ~48%. Monster draw.

potOdds.prompt: You have 5c6c on 7d8hKc. OESD. Straight draw outs?
potOdds.explanation: OESD: four 4s + four 9s = 8 clean outs. The backdoor flush is bonus ~4% — don't count as main equity.

potOdds.prompt: You have AsKd on KhTc8c. TPTK vs suspected flush draw. Outs to boat/trips?
potOdds.explanation: 3 remaining Ks (trips/boat) + 3 remaining As (two pair ahead of draws) = ~7 meaningful outs to improve.

potOdds.prompt: Jh9h on KhTh2c (flush draw, 9 outs). Villain bets $50 into $100. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 50/150 = 33%. Flush draw: 9x4 = ~36% equity. More equity than needed — easy call. Raising for protection also valid.

potOdds.prompt: 8c7c on 9d5hAc (OESD, 8 outs). Villain bets $20 into $80. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 20/100 = 20%. OESD: 8x4 = ~32% equity. Outstanding odds — calling is mandatory.

potOdds.prompt: Qc9c on Kd8c3c (flush draw, 9 outs). Villain bets $90 into $120. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 90/210 = 43%. Flush draw: ~36% equity. Close but you have backdoor equity + future bluff equity. Call or semi-bluff raise are both valid.

potOdds.prompt: Td9d on Jd7c2d (flush draw + OESD, ~12 outs). Villain pots $150. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 33%. 12 outs x 4 = ~48% equity. You're a FAVORITE — raise. You have near-even equity and can force a mistake with a re-raise.

potOdds.prompt: 6h5h on Ah8d3c (OESD, 8 outs, backdoor hearts). Villain pots $200. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 33%. OESD: 32% equity. Essentially break-even, but villain potting on A-high usually means strength. Marginally fold.

potOdds.prompt: Ac4c on Kc8c2d (nut flush draw, 9 outs). Villain bets $100 into $300. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 25%. Nut flush draw: ~36% equity + ace blocker + fold equity. Way profitable. Raise as a semi-bluff.

potOdds.prompt: Villain bets $33 into $100 (~1/3 pot). Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 33/133 = 25%. A third-pot bet gives 3:1 odds. Most 8+ out draws are profitable.

potOdds.prompt: Villain overbets 2x pot ($1000 into $500). Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 1000/1500 = 67%. Overbets price out almost everything. Unless near top of range, fold.

potOdds.prompt: AsJs on Ks9s4c (nut flush draw, 9 outs). Villain bets $40 into $100. Call or raise?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 29%. Nut flush: ~36% equity. Profitable to call, but raising is better — nut draw + fold equity + can win immediately.

potOdds.prompt: Quick: villain bets $25 into $100. Required equity?
potOdds.explanation: 25/125 = 20%. Quarter-pot bet gives 4:1 odds. Almost any 8+ out draw is profitable.

potOdds.prompt: 8h7h on 9h6d2h. OESD + flush draw (~15 outs). Villain bets $140 into $200. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 41%. 15 outs x 4 = ~60% equity. You're a clear FAVORITE — raise for value and protection.

potOdds.prompt: KcQc on Ac5c2h. Flush draw (non-nut). How many outs?
potOdds.explanation: Still 9 flush outs. K-high flush loses to A-high — be careful calling big bets with a non-nut flush draw.

potOdds.prompt: Jh9h on Th8h2s. OESD + flush draw (~15 outs). Villain overbets $600 into $400. Call?
potOdds.explanation: Pot odds: 60%. 15 outs x 4 = ~60% equity. Near exact break-even but you have straight flush outs as bonus. Raise all-in — you have the best of it.

comboDrills.prompt: AhKh in a 3-bet pot on Jh8h2c. Primary equity source?
comboDrills.explanation: AKhh = 2 overcards + nut flush draw. ~39% equity. Raising hand, not a check-back.

comboDrills.prompt: Same spot but AsKd (no hearts) on Jh8h2c. How does equity change vs AhKh?
comboDrills.explanation: AKo without hearts = just 2 overcards, ~26% equity. Lost ~13% vs suited version. Suits matter enormously on draw-heavy boards.

comboDrills.prompt: AcQc on KcTc5d. What makes this hand especially strong?
comboDrills.explanation: AcQc = nut flush draw (9 outs) + gutshot to J (4 outs) + ace overcard. ~50%+ equity. Semi-bluff raise, not passive call.

comboDrills.prompt: Ah5s on Kd7c2h OOP (you're BB). Main strategic use of the ace?
comboDrills.explanation: Ah5s on K72 = nothing made. But the ACE blocks villain's AK, AA — hands most likely to c-bet. This enables a check-raise bluff.

comboDrills.prompt: 3-betting JcTc — where is this hand most profitable?
comboDrills.explanation: JTs thrives in position. 3-betting BTN vs CO is excellent. From UTG it's too loose — you'd be OOP against all callers.

comboDrills.prompt: KcQc in 3-bet pot on JcTc3s. How do you play this?
comboDrills.explanation: KcQc = straight draw + nut flush draw + overcards. ~50%+ equity. Check-raise semi-bluff — max equity + fold equity. Don't passive-call.
Top set but nightmare board — rundown AND two-tone. Multiple draws have major equity. Bet big and protect.

comboDrills.prompt: AdJd on KsQd2d. Called a 3-bet OOP. What makes this hand tricky?

options.label: Strong draw but dominated by made hands

comboDrills.explanation: AdJd = nut flush draw (~40% equity) BUT AK, KQ, AQ dominate your gutshot completers in a 3-bet pot.
Small pairs need 10:1 implied odds to set-mine. At 100bb deep and in position, 22 can call a 3-bet. Shallow stacks or OOP = fold.

comboDrills.prompt: KhQh on Kd9s4h (top pair + backdoor hearts). Default?
comboDrills.explanation: TPTK on a dry board = small bet to build pot. No draws to charge. Small bet lets worse hands call.

comboDrills.prompt: As2s on KsJd8s. What does this hand do best?

options.label: Nut flush draw is main equity
Nut flush draw + ace blocker combo

comboDrills.explanation: As2s = nut flush draw (9 outs) + ace blocker to villain's top pair/AK range. Premium semi-bluff — check-raise aggressively.

comboDrills.prompt: QsJs on Qd7h2c. What does suitedness add beyond top pair?

options.label: Two pair on a brick turn
Top pair + backdoor flush + backdoor straight

comboDrills.explanation: QJss on Q72r = TPTK + backdoor flush + backdoor straight. All add small equity. Value bet all streets.

comboDrills.prompt: AcKs on Ac5d2h. Villain check-raises your small c-bet. Read?

options.label: Depends on player type and stack depth

comboDrills.explanation: AK on A52r is strong but villain's check-raise range varies by player. Against fish = often weaker, against tight reg at 100bb it's sets + draws. Stack depth drives the decision.

comboDrills.prompt: 5s4s on As3s2c. Flopped wheel straight + nut flush draw. Best move?
comboDrills.explanation: Nut straight + nut flush redraw = top of range. Don't slow-play. Bet big (75-100% pot) and stack off if raised.

comboDrills.prompt: AhQd on KhJd5c. Gutshot to T, OOP in 3-bet pot. Best line?
comboDrills.explanation: AQo OOP with gutshot in 3-bet pot = ~26% equity. Enough to call one bet, not enough to raise. Check-call flop, re-evaluate turn.
